title: Kernel-SDF: An Open-Source Library for Real-Time Signed Distance Function Estimation using Kernel Regression
abstract: 精确高效的环境表征对于运动规划、操作与导航等机器人应用至关重要。有符号距离函数（SDF）已成为编码障碍物边界距离的强大表征方式，能够支持高效的碰撞检测与轨迹优化技术。然而，现有SDF重建方法在处理流式传感器数据的大规模不确定性感知SDF估计时存在局限：基于体素的方法受限于固定分辨率且缺乏不确定性量化，神经网络方法需要大量训练时间，而高斯过程（GP）方法则在可扩展性、符号估计和不确定性校准方面面临挑战。本文提出一个开源库Kernel-SDF，利用核回归方法实现具有校准不确定性量化的实时SDF学习。该方法包含通过核回归学习连续占据场的前端模块，以及利用前端表面边界样本通过GP回归估计精确SDF的后端模块。Kernel-SDF能够实时提供精确的SDF值、SDF梯度、SDF不确定性和网格构建功能。评估结果表明，Kernel-SDF在保持实时性能的同时，相比现有方法实现了更高的精度，适用于需要可靠不确定性感知几何信息的各类机器人应用场景。

Kernel-SDF: An Open-Source Library for Real-Time Signed Distance Function Estimation using Kernel Regression
Zhirui Dai
1,*
Tianxing Fan
1,*
Mani Amani
1
Jaemin Seo
2
Ki Myung Brian Lee
1
Hyondong Oh
2
Nikolay Atanasov
1
*
Equal contribution
1
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, University of California, San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093 USA. Email: {zhdai,t2fan,mamai5250,kmblee,natanasov}@ucsd.edu
2
Department of Mechanical Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), Daejeon 34051, Republic of Korea. Email: {jam.seo,h.oh}@kaist.ac.kr
Abstract
Accurate and efficient environment representation is crucial for robotic applications such as motion planning, manipulation, and navigation. Signed distance functions (SDFs) have emerged as a powerful representation for encoding distance to obstacle boundaries, enabling efficient collision-checking and trajectory optimization techniques.
However, existing SDF reconstruction methods have limitations when it comes to large-scale uncertainty-aware SDF estimation from streaming sensor data. Voxel-based approaches are limited by fixed resolution and lack uncertainty quantification, neural network methods require significant training time, while Gaussian process (GP) methods struggle with scalability, sign estimation, and uncertainty calibration.
In this letter, we develop an open-source library, Kernel-SDF, which uses kernel regression to learn SDF with calibrated uncertainty quantification in real-time.
Our approach consists of a front-end that learns a continuous occupancy field via kernel regression, and a back-end that estimates accurate SDF via GP regression using samples from the front-end surface boundaries.
Kernel-SDF provides accurate SDF, SDF gradient, SDF uncertainty, and mesh construction in real-time. Evaluation results show that Kernel-SDF achieves superior accuracy compared to existing methods, while maintaining real-time performance, making it suitable for various robotics applications requiring reliable uncertainty-aware geometric information.
I
Introduction
Geometric environment representations play a key role for many robot autonomy capabilities, including mapping, localization, motion planning, manipulation, and navigation. An ideal representation should meet several requirements:
1)
accuracy to reflect the environment structure,
2)
computational efficiency to allow real-time processing of streaming sensor information,
3)
scalability to capture large environments,
4)
robustness to noise,
5)
uncertainty quantification to support risk-aware decision making,
6)
differentiability to support gradient-based optimization,
7)
adaptability to dynamic environments, and
8)
compatibility with different sensors.
Various effective representations have been proposed in the literature: occupancy data structures
[
6
]
, point clouds
[
3
,
16
,
39
]
, meshes
[
33
,
26
]
, and implicit functions such as signed distance function (SDF)
[
20
,
21
,
25
,
5
,
10
,
38
,
34
,
29
,
32
,

,

]
and neural radiance field (NeRF)
[
17
]
.
Among these, SDFs have attracted attention due to their ability to provide the distance to the nearest obstacles, which is useful for mapping
[
20
,
30
]
and motion planning
[
11
,
12
]
. Furthermore, differentiable SDF methods can be used to define safety constraints in navigation
[
14
]
and manipulation
[
13
]
.

Various methods have been proposed to learn SDFs from range sensors, such as LiDAR or depth camera. Voxel-based methods
[
20
,
21
,
5
,
32
]
often focus only on truncated SDF (TSDF) estimation but some
[
21
,
5
,
2
,
18
]
are able to reconstruct non-truncated (Euclidean) SDF on a discrete grid.
These methods maintain a dense discrete approximation of (T)SDF with fixed resolution, which leads to a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency and prevents differentiability.
In contrast, neural network methods
[
25
,
22
,
35
,
8
,
24
,
30
]
learn SDF as a continuous function, avoiding an explicit grid representation and providing accurate and differentiable approximations.
However, these methods are not suitable for online learning due to the significant training time needed to converge to a good SDF estimate.
Gaussian process (GP) methods
[
10
,
38
,
41
,
37
]
are non-parametric and capable of estimating accurate Euclidean SDF.
However, existing GP methods scale poorly with increasing environment sizes, lack robust sign estimation, and report variance estimates that are inconsistent with actual SDF prediction errors.
To enable scalability, octree
[
38
]
and OpenVDB
[
37
]
data structures are used to split the training data into smaller chunks for training multiple GPs.
To provide consistent sign and variance estimates, GMMGP
[
41
]
uses an SDF prior from a hierarchical Gaussian mixture model but this prior provides increasingly larger errors as the query position moves further away from the surface.
In this letter, we develop an open-source library, Kernel-SDF, for SDF estimation, meeting the aforementioned requirements for an effective geometric representation and addressing the limitations of the existing GP methods. To achieve this, Kernel-SDF maintains an octree data structure to split the estimation process into smaller areas, estimates the occupancy/sign in each octant using a Bayesian Hilbert map (BHM)
[
27
]
, and reconstructs SDF via GP regression from the BHM surface samples. Thus, the library is organized as a surface estimation front-end, performing BHM occupancy estimation, and an SDF prediction back-end, preforming GP regression. The front-end learns a continuous occupancy field for robust sign estimation and provides surface point samples via the marching cubes algorithm
[
15
]
. The back-end uses the surface point samples to train SDF GPs in each octant with integrated uncertainty quantification. We introduce several optimizations to ensure real-time performance and map consistency: spatial partitioning and priority-queue-based updates for efficient computation and BHM weight synchronization to maintain consistency across neighboring local maps.
Kernel-SDF provides accurate SDF, SDF gradient, SDF uncertainty, and mesh construction in real time (see Fig.
1
). In summary, our contributions include:
1.
an open-source C++ implementation
1
1
1
Link will be available after acceptance.
for 2D/3D SDF learning, featuring Python and ROS1/ROS2 interfaces with extensive real-time testing;
2.
a highly-optimized kernel regression method for real-time SDF learning via two components: BHM front-end for continuous occupancy learning and uncertainty-aware surface extraction, and GP back-end for SDF and SDF gradient prediction with uncertainty;
3.
five strategies to achieve efficiency and robustness: octree-based partitioning for local region learning, optimized data processing for rapid training set generation, weight synchronization for map consistency, adaptive scaling for GP stability, and priority-queue-based updates to balance accuracy with responsiveness.
II
Related Work
SDF learning methods can be categorized as voxel-based, neural-net-based, and Gaussian-process-based.
II-A
Voxel Methods
Many methods have been proposed to learn SDF using a grid
[
4
]
.
KinectFusion
[
20
]
pioneered real-time 3D reconstruction using depth sensors based on projective TSDF.
Follow-up works, such as Voxblox
[
21
]
, extended this to Euclidean SDF by breadth-first-search (BFS) integration from the occupied voxels. However, projective TSDF estimates the true SDF as distance along camera rays, which is inaccurate, and BFS integration introduces additional errors.
FIESTA
[
5
]
integrates SDF estimation with an occupancy map, replacing the BFS-based distance with the distance to the nearest occupied voxel.
VoxField
[
23
]
formulates non-projective TSDF, though its accuracy relies on the quality of estimated surface normals and SDF gradients.
A common limitation among these methods is the use of fixed-resolution grids, which restricts scalability in larger environments.
VDBblox
[
2
]
and nvblox
[
18
]
address scalability by using OpenVDB
[
19
]
or GPU-based voxel hashing.
However, voxel-based methods lack uncertainty quantification and are usually not differentiable.
II-B
Neural Network Methods
Neural network methods offer differentiability by design and can achieve highly accurate SDF reconstruction, as shown by DeepSDF
[
25
]
. DeepSDF uses an MLP with latent features to learn SDF of objects.
NeuS
[
34
]
and NeuS2
[
35
]
combine SDF with neural radiance fields (NeRF)
[
17
]
to jointly learn SDF and rendering. PIN-SLAM
[
24
]
and MISO
[
30
]
implement simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) based on an SDF map encoded using neural features.
H
2
\text{H}_{2}
-Mapping
[
8
]
uses a voxel SDF prior and a neural TSDF residual, which are co-optimized with volumetric rendering.
However, most neural network methods do not meet real-time requirements for operating in novel environments due to their significant training time. Although some works, like
H
2
\text{H}_{2}
-Mapping
[
8
]
and PIN-SLAM
[
24
]
, achieve neural SDF learning online, they sacrifice the SDF accuracy very much.
II-C
Gaussian Process Methods
GP methods are suitable for online SDF learning but face challenges related to the high computational cost of kernel matrix inversion as the environment scale increases.
To mitigate this, GPIS
[
10
]
employs octree-based data partitioning and trains multiple GPs for each local partition. However, its SDF estimates quickly revert to the zero-mean prior away from the surface.
Log-GPIS
[
38
]
observes an important connection between the heat kernel and the unsigned distance function, and formulates unsigned distance field GP regression in log space.
However, the log transformation does not consider sign information and distorts variance estimation, causing uncertainty to explode far from the surface. Furthermore, the surface estimation in Log-GPIS is sensitive to sensor noise and dynamic environmental changes.
VDB-GPDF
[
37
]
improves surface estimation through OpenVDB TSDF fusion but does not provide sign estimates or reliable uncertainty quantification.
To overcome these limitations, we introduce a continuous occupancy field front-end in Kernel-SDF based on BHM
[
7
,
27
]
, which provides robust sign prediction and accurate surface estimation, even in dynamic or noisy environments. Our GP back-end, then, learns SDF from the surface samples and formulates an uncertainty estimate that respects the SDF errors to deliver precise SDF and gradient predictions with reliable uncertainty quantification.

Kernel-SDF
Our approach, Kernel-SDF, consists of a surface estimation front-end using BHM
[
27
]
(Sec.
IV-A
) and an SDF prediction back-end using GP regression
[
36

Both BHMs and GPs are kernel regression methods that support continuous representations with uncertainty quantification.
However, directly applying kernel regression over the entire environment is computationally expensive and impractical for real-time applications.
Kernel-SDF addresses this by partitioning the environment into multiple local regions, defined by an octree data structure, and applying kernel regression within each region with additional optimization techniques.

IV-C
Priority Queue Based Update
Beyond the core BHM and GP optimizations, we also use priority-queue-based update to improve the efficiency.
Frequent sensor measurements make running surface extraction, updating GP training data buffer, and GP training after every BHM update computationally wasteful.
To optimize performance, we delay these processes until necessary using a three-queue priority system shown in Fig.
3
.
To stay responsive, frequently queried GPs get higher priority for data buffer updates and retraining.
Besides, BHMs with the oldest marching request timestamps are prioritized to process regions that are likely to remain stable.
This strategy ensures computational resources are focused on high-demand areas while maintaining overall map correctness.

To isolate distance accuracy from occupancy misclassification (e.g., by FIESTA), we compare the absolute values of predicted and ground-truth SDFs.
Table
III
summarizes these results. Kernel-SDF consistently achieves the lowest MAE for both SDF values and gradients across nearly all datasets and regions.
While FIESTA is competitive on the Replica dataset, its accuracy degrades significantly on Cow and Lady and Newer College due to sensor noise.
Voxblox and iSDF exhibit higher errors, likely due to voxel discretizations and over-smoothing, respectively.
Notably, while VDB-GPDF outperforms Voxblox in distance accuracy, its gradient MAE is the highest among all methods, indicating poor gradient estimation.
Our method’s superior performance in the "Near" region opens the possibility for applications to manipulation and navigation, while its accurate long-range predictions and gradients may facilitate path planning and optimization-based control.

We evaluate efficiency by measuring the average per-frame SDF update time and the prediction latency for 1k query positions. As shown in Table
IV
, Kernel-SDF consistently achieves real-time performance with an average update time of approximately 150 ms across varying scene scales.
While Voxblox builds TSDF rapidly, its BFS-based SDF integration is slowed down by the high volume of voxel updates.
FIESTA achieves the fastest integration. However, as shown in Sec.
V-A
and
V-B
, its accuracy is not satisfactory in noisy environments.
iSDF remains slow due to its multi-iteration convergence requirements and the overhead of analytical gradient backpropagation.
Although VDB-GPDF shares our log-GP representation, its reliance on VDB structures for spatial partitioning results in significantly higher update latencies.
Overall, Kernel-SDF strikes a good balance between computational speed and mapping precision, providing a robust solution for real-time robotic applications.

V-F
Autonomous Robot Navigation Case Study
(a)
Jackal robot
(b)
SDF and safe bubble cover
Figure 10
:
Kernel-SDF enables safe bubble cover construction
[
11
]
and safe robot navigation. (a) A Clearpath Jackal robot equipped with a LiDAR sensor navigating in a laboratory environment. (b) Visualization of the real-time Kernel-SDF estimate as a heatmap, the safe bubble cover generated from it (union of orange circles), and the planned trajectory (black line).
Real-time, uncertainty-quantified SDF mapping is essential for safe robot navigation. We demonstrate this by constructing a safe navigation corridor from a chain of overlapping bubbles, by planning in the SDF map using the sampling-based approach proposed in
[
11
]
.
The planner constructs a rapidly exploring random tree from sampled positions in free space with associated safe bubbles. The radii are specified by the SDF values minus a safety margin, which can be scaled by the estimated SDF uncertainty.
As shown in Fig.
10
, the planner recovers a connected chain of SDF bubbles spanning the start and goal positions, ensuring the path remains strictly within the mapped free space. This case study validates the utility of Kernel-SDF in providing the accurate distance and uncertainty information required for risk-aware motion planning.
VI
Conclusion
We presented Kernel-SDF, a real-time kernel regression framework for differentiable SDF learning with uncertainty quantification. By coupling BHMs for robust surface estimation with GPs for distance prediction, our method achieves superior reconstruction accuracy and efficiency. The hierarchical tree structure ensures scalability for large-scale environments, while our softmin-based uncertainty quantification enables risk-aware decision-making for downstream robotic tasks.
Extensive evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that Kernel-SDF outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in both quality and speed. Furthermore, our BHM-based front-end effectively mitigates sensor noise and adapts to dynamic environments, providing a robust and practical solution for real-time robotic applications.

VII-D
Priority-Based Update Scheme
In Kernel-SDF, several operations take significant time, including BHM EM update, BHM marching, GP training data collection, and GP training. However, not all operations need to be performed at every time step. Therefore, we introduce a priority-based update scheme to efficiently allocate computational resources to the most needed updates.
To maintain the responsiveness of the system to the latest sensor data, BHM EM updates are performed at every time step for all BHMs that have new sensor data. Newly created BHMs also need to be marched immediately to get surface points for its corresponding GP training data buffer update. For all GP training operations, we delay them until they are required for SDF prediction.
For other operations, we use priority queues to determine the order of updates based on their necessity.
In order to make the system also responsive to the latest query trends (e.g., the down-stream planner may focus on a specific area), each GP has a query counter
c
0
c_{0}
that records how many times the GP is requested for SDF prediction. The counter helps to reshape the priority of GP training and data buffer updates. When a GP is trained, its query counter is reduced by half. And the query counter is capped to a maximum value (10000) to avoid over-prioritization.

Efficient Tree Construction
To optimize occupancy mapping, we extend the Octomap

This approach leverages spatial locality that when a free voxel is updated, its neighboring voxels are more likely to be updated, improving the CPU cache hit rate.

VII-F
3
SDF Metrics
For SDF evaluation, we query each method with a regular grid of points covering the bounding box of the ground truth mesh or point cloud. The grid resolution is set to 0.05m for the Replica and Cow&Lady datasets, and 0.2m for the Newer College dataset. Prediction may fail for Voxblox and FIESTA at some query points because they rely on the eight voxel corners to interpolate the SDF value. In such cases, we exclude the failed points when calculating the metrics for Voxblox and FIESTA.
VII-F
4
Time Metrics
While it is straightforward to measure the prediction time for all methods, measuring the update time is more complex due to the different update strategies employed by each method. For a fair comparison, we measure the average update time per scan for all methods. Specifically, we record the total time taken to process all scans in a dataset (but exclude time of data loading, logging and etc.) and divide it by the number of scans to obtain the average update time per scan. This approach provides a consistent basis for comparing the efficiency of each method’s update process.
preview_text:
Accurate and efficient environment representation is crucial for robotic applications such as motion planning, manipulation, and navigation. Signed distance functions (SDFs) have emerged as a powerful representation for encoding distance to obstacle boundaries, enabling efficient collision-checking and trajectory optimization techniques. However, existing SDF reconstruction methods have limitations when it comes to large-scale uncertainty-aware SDF estimation from streaming sensor data. Voxel-based approaches are limited by fixed resolution and lack uncertainty quantification, neural network methods require significant training time, while Gaussian process (GP) methods struggle with scalability, sign estimation, and uncertainty calibration. In this letter, we develop an open-source library, Kernel-SDF, which uses kernel regression to learn SDF with calibrated uncertainty quantification in real-time. Our approach consists of a front-end that learns a continuous occupancy field via kernel regression, and a back-end that estimates accurate SDF via GP regression using samples from the front-end surface boundaries. Kernel-SDF provides accurate SDF, SDF gradient, SDF uncertainty, and mesh construction in real-time. Evaluation results show that Kernel-SDF achieves superior accuracy compared to existing methods, while maintaining real-time performance, making it suitable for various robotics applications requiring reliable uncertainty-aware geometric information.

Kernel-SDF: An Open-Source Library for Real-Time Signed Distance Function Estimation using Kernel Regression
